title: The UK will scan asylum-seekers' faces for age checks—despite knowing the tech is flawed
gist: A WIRED/Lighthouse Reports investigation reveals the UK plans to deploy facial age estimation AI at its border to assess asylum-seekers' ages from 2027, despite a leaked Home Office report showing the systems regularly misjudge children as adults and exhibit significant racial bias against Sub-Saharan Africans.

themes.theme: Facial age estimation accuracy and bias

insights.claim: A leaked Home Office report found its best-performing facial age estimation algorithm had 'substantial deviations' on Sub-Saharan Africans — for female Sub-Saharan Africans the age guess was off by an average of 4.6 years, so a 13.5-year-old girl could be assessed as an 18-year-old adult.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Documents severe demographic bias in deployed-grade FAE; a regulatory and reputational risk for vendors and governments adopting the tech.

insights.claim: In controlled lab tests the best FAE algorithms predict age to within ~2.5 years, but accuracy varies wildly with gender, demographics, and image quality, and poor-quality images drastically reduce performance (some systems fooled by video-game character images).
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Lab accuracy overstates real-world performance; image-quality dependence is a fundamental limitation for high-stakes deployment.

insights.claim: The Home Office tested seven FAE algorithms against more than 2.5 million images using high-quality photos, meaning real-world accuracy on low-quality first-encounter border photos would likely be even worse — and trauma/stress of travel ('temporary aging') appeared to further degrade accuracy.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Operational conditions systematically worsen the technology's already-flawed accuracy, undermining its core justification.
insights.locator: The leaked Home Office report produced in April 2025

insights.claim: The UK spent more than $400,000 in May 2026 on face-scanning technology from German firm Cognitec, one of the seven tested algorithms, with rollout delayed to 2027 to 'crack down on fake claims.'
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Named vendor and contract value for a controversial government AI deployment; a procurement signal for the FAE vendor market.
insights.locator: It is unclear if the flawed system

insights.claim: WIRED analysis found Cognitec's system misclassified twice as many 16-year-olds as 18-or-older on low-quality border photos versus higher-quality visa photos, and NIST data showed West African 16-year-olds were more likely than Eastern European ones to be misclassified as adults.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Independent corroboration of demographic and image-quality bias in the procured vendor's system.
insights.locator: A WIRED and Lighthouse Reports analysis

insights.claim: The Home Office disbanded its scientific advisory committee on age estimation while exploring AI adoption, with a former member calling the face scans 'hideously inaccurate' and saying their concerns were never presented before the committee was shut down.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Governance red flag: independent expert oversight was removed during adoption, raising accountability and legal-challenge risk.

insights.claim: Facial age estimation, already spreading through online age-verification mandates (social media bans, porn restrictions, retail), is moving into high-stakes offline use, with this UK border deployment believed to be the first of its kind for asylum age checks.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Signals an expanding addressable market for age-estimation AI vendors driven by regulation, alongside mounting bias-related liability.
